speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was made out of bone. And you couldn't very well lure them. Because they were... They were real bone die. They had cut the corners off too. They were doing a lot of things here in Padlars too. And on way up there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They were...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Wood legend and gambling and doing everything. Italians were wood legend, not the Greeks. The Greeks, they didn't ever dig wood. Wood like because they didn't have no places of their own, you see. Up the hallway... Now one Italian was here and was wood legend, but the... The others were up the hallway. And they were doing it there because it was a different town, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say places of their own, what do you mean? A place, a house to live in that was out of town?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not out of town, up down way. You know, around... But not in Padlars. Well, in Padlars too, but not very much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In Padlars.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because they were... They didn't arrest it. They had some wine and drinking wine for themselves. And it used to come from... From Moscow, you know. The sheriff used to come around and raid in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Summerfield?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, Summerfield. I've heard about him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You don't remember him. No, he's dead. He's been dead for a long time. But I know Hap Moody. He was one of that bunch. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Hap Moody is in the home now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, that's all right. He's 90 years old. Hap, he said, I know him pretty well. He's a good fellow. He's pretty smart. Pretty smart...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: What you call...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And not a big fellow sheriff. He just died here not very long ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jordan?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Jordan.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I've heard about Summerfield stopping a lot of people, but not about arrests. I don't hear about many arrests. No, they didn't arrest him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They just warned him and scared him out.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And how is this fellow that we had here before?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He went down here once in a while. He lost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The last year?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, he died. He was sick, you know, and he passed away just lately.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean Jordan?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, not Jordan.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: After Jordan.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know. It's not Summerfield. You're thinking of more recent. No, not Summerfield. He was just recently.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He used to come over here with Bob Felton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know now?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I don't know the name, but I know who you mean.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Felton is a lawyer, you see. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think he's prosecuting a attorney now, isn't he?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: it's not... Now that Lérygé was, but now it's... Well, Lérygé is out.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He used to fight with them, what you call the commissioners. But he didn't run. He didn't run the last time, you see. He didn't... And I think... I think Felton is prosecuting a attorney. If I remember, I don't know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: This guy that's prosecuting attorney now, he has a beard. I know that. He's a pretty young fellow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, the one who was prosecuting attorney for the county.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is his name?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I can't think of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say the Greeks didn't bootleg and that was because they didn't have their own homes?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, they didn't have their own home.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But the high -tilers, one of the federal fellows that had family too, you see. Mike Petruolo, he used to be one of the ones who used to bootleg. He was arrested even in the jail for comforting the ones.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But... He had his own place, his own family. He was selling groceries too, you see. He was backing bread too himself.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And... solid. Was... No, the Greeks didn't have no... They never did bootleg. Very... One, he used to bring bottles of beer from Spokane. And he was selling once in a while, but not in a... Good high -scale, not in a big scale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people buy from the same bootlegger all the time if they could? Or would they buy just from wherever they could get it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They could sell whatever they could get it if they wanted to. They didn't care who they buy it from. But they knew who was handling that stuff, you see. And they went over there to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't some of it a lot better than others?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I heard some of it was pretty bad, some of it was really good. As far as quality, how good it was.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, the quality.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, different lot. Yeah, you mean...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wussy?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. Well, different... Some of it was good, and some of it was not good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the heck is that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Sounds like he's across the... The way here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The Donca here, he wants it one.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I've heard that in Spokane they had a lot of houses, too, a prostitution there. Well, prostitution, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was wide open in Spokane at one time.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But now, I don't think they are, because I've never been in Spokane now for years. I go in for business, you know, in and out.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know anything about it, but... Because I used to go now for the eyes, you see how these girls walk in. And Reverend Anderson took me up there, and he means... His wife, Drog, he can drive himself. He can drive around town here, but not in Spokane. His wife is a good driver. And I used to go with him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I took a few trips for the fire pressure. And I didn't have good luck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was the place that you gambled? Was that Durkin's or was that some other place?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I heard of a place called Durkin's up there. Durkin was saloon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where people gambled?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not only one place. They were wide open. All the saloons had a lot of places on gambling. They were tablets, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they even now, or lately, they had... They allowed gambling in some places.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But I don't think they do now.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because I don't know. I don't know what they do now. Well, I'm more curious about the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they had prostitution, would the girls just come down in the saloons? Is that where they'd pick up the men?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, no.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, they were in certain places.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, houses, yeah. Yeah, sure.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They did around in the street. Oh, there are some out in the street, too, but not too many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it true that they used to come down into this area from Spokane?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Sometimes. Sometimes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Sometimes, fellas, you know, that knew them. They'd bring you over here. Here was a lot of people living here, you see. Now it's dead. They had thrown two ships over there, and they were on the machinery. It took place of people, now, of men. And there are not very many men. They used to work a thousand or more, fifteen hundred people, you see. They had another street here, where we lived. Greeks and Italians. They called it the International Street. But they took them houses during the war. And because there was no, nobody to random, you see. And one fella, he's still living here. He took a contract and he hauled them away, and sold them for whatever he did. They didn't want to burn them down, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the working day? Was it a ten hour day when you were first here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, ten hours a day, up to when we had that wobbly strike, whatever they called that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The IWW, huh?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: IWW.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did that do?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, after that, that didn't do very much, no, because it didn't last very long. They formed the Law of the Legion of Loggers and Lumberman. And everybody joined up, you see. And gradually, they brought the eight hour work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who got?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Pretty soon, from the eight hour work. Before that, we just go down there half an hour earlier to go on the machine. You see, those ones that had the machine thrown, they had to all of themselves. And after that, why they had a man to go on, you didn't have to do anything except on a husher of blue. You were ready to go to work and ready to get out on a machine at four o 'clock. They had different business. It was strictly eight hours work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But ten hours, we worked for ten hours there for two dollars a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two dollars a day?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, I worked for two dollars a day here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But there were dollars. You see, three dollars you could buy a pair of shoes. But now you can't do that. You see, the dollar was worth something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With this ten hour a day, did you ever have overtime besides?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, you couldn't.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They didn't have no overtime on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it have you worked?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because they were working ten hours, you know, and you couldn't very well have overtime to work for thirteen hours. Unless, in some places, you probably worked during the holidays, in the cold winter months, on the machinery, froze. Now there we had about half a day in Thanksgiving Day and half a day sometimes in Christmas.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: To keep the thing running, you see, keep it from freezing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean it would freeze up if it wasn't used?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, it would. Sure, the machinery, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Now they are freezing. They had conveyors, you know, with chains. And pretty soon if it was frozen, or as soon as you started when it was frozen, they crystallized and they broke.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had trouble with that. Now they had different machinery, you see, they are all machinery. They had machinery, they had a burner here. Or they burned the west things that they didn't want to use, you see, slabs and things like that. Well, they had trouble with that because it was clear outside, you see. The conveyor was in open and that will once in a while, it will break and they have to fix it. It was different altogether. And now it's different altogether down there, even now.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because I haven't been down there for close to 20 years, you see, I've retired. And I don't know very much about it, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You haven't been down to visit it or see what it's like?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I've been down a couple of times in the early days.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That is after I retired, but since then I... I get down there and look. I went down there and I had... Somebody from Greece came over here and I took him down there to show him. And I had to go down, but it wasn't much change then, you see. But now they... It's a lot of changes. It's unbelievable to me.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: What it was, three men. It's only one man and he's got a buttons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've been down there in the mill?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I've been at Bennett's, but I've never been here at Potlatch to see the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You haven't?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I've seen the Bennett mill in Princeton. That's smaller. Well, that's all right. That's modern mill.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's more modern than this, I think. Because they just built that. Bennett built them.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Boone. And since Bennett took it over, he remodeled it. You know, it's where that man there, the head saw it. He's only on any punch buttons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked a ten hour day, did you have to work all ten hours? Could you take breaks easily or what?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Unless you had to go to toilet or something like that. Otherwise you work straight ten hours.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they couldn't stop you from that. They had a man. They had a man that was cleaning up, you know, and he took your place. But he couldn't take... He couldn't take everybody's place. It was a little bit different then. They had two, three men cleaning up. Now they don't have anybody, you see. The whistle blows and you go to smoke. They allow you ten minutes for a coffee break.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's all.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And you have to be there when... You notice the whistle when they blow sometimes. During two o 'clock hour, two o 'clock and two, ten minutes after two, you have to be there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they supervise you very closely?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Watch what the men did pretty close.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, not very close. The foreman was a little bit different man. You see, he washed him, yes, because some of the fellas, you know, they lay down. You know, they loosen up on their work, you see. They didn't do their work as they should be. But he couldn't watch everybody.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The foreman was the whole thing then. He could fire and hire, but now he can. You know, the fireman has to go to... The man that will fire, he goes to the union and complain and they have to put him back or find out the reason why he is fired.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: See, they have different...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that a good way of doing it for the foreman to fire and hire whenever you wanted?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was a good and bad because if the foreman had a friend, you know, he never fired him. He fired somebody else. But then he fired him and put somebody else that is friendly to the foreman. And that way is no good, but the union, I think, is better. Although I never did join the union when I was here while I was working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, there was different things. There were many things that... I joined the union on the first come, the AFO -VEL, and then came the CIO and they got the bargaining agency and they wanted me to join that. And I said, no, I didn't join that because I enjoyed the AFO -VEL and they lost out. But now, of course, it's a combined AFO -VEL and CIO and everything is peaceful. At that time, they were fighting one another.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The AFO -VEL and the CIO fought each other. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think the AFO -VEL was better or did it make a difference?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I mean in those days.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, in those days, the AFO -VEL came first, you see, and I joined them. And then they lost the bargaining agency and they wanted me to join the CIO and I didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the IWW?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, well, the IWW was a radical organization anyway. It didn't last very long. It was too radical to exist. They were fighting against the capital, you see, and you can do that because the mail here, the IWW, they have to strike and get somebody to work there because they have to run, I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because they have to what?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They have to run the sawmill, you see. They didn't want to get some of Ed Musso's talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get some of Ed Musso's?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I'm going to. Next time I see him, when I come back, see him. His wife is a very nice person, too, interesting person, too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, she's not...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That they were, when you say against capital, the IWW, what do you mean they were, what is that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I love yesterday they were against the capitalism because they were trying to do the things that they wanted to do, you see, and that was too much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They demanded a lot from the capital. And the capitalists, of course, they didn't do, they couldn't stand for that. Now it's different because they both agree to some things. They don't always agree, but they have a contract. They sign a contract and they have to abide by the agreement of the contract, whatever they do. It's different altogether. And at that time they didn't have no contract. They didn't know nothing. The IWW organized it first. And they wanted to, I never did join the IWW. I don't know very much about it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they did try to shut the mill down in Elke, over there, and they run them out and they told them they either have to go to work or go to an army. That was before World War I. And some did go to army. But others, they left, they built. I know one Greek fellow there that he wasn't, he didn't go to either army or anything, but he even changed his name, you know, and then they couldn't, you know, they couldn't find nothing against him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, he wasn't, he wasn't the leader.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the leader went to the army. And of course after he came over, the army, he started business in gambling house in Spokane.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And his son, he was general manager of the horses, who are the guardians of Spokane, George Manos.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But I saw him holding the radio the other day, or he retired from that position and he's got something else now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is the father dead now?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, you haven't. I knew him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, he believed in the IWW then?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, he was IWW.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Then after IWW was defunct, there was nothing, then he didn't care anything about IWW because he opened a gambling house and he had other business, other business than working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a guy was he?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, he was a good man.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: My son.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But he was a businessman.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was a friend of yours. He pretended to be a friend. And he was a friend to a certain extent. But then when it's, if he could, he would take the money away from you.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was a pretty good card player.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I thought the IWWs would be against businessmen, in the big business anyway. At that time it wasn't at the big business, but they were in a big business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean in a business, what do they employ?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: A lot of people. Well, yeah, that's all right. But that place didn't employ very many. It was just gambling joint.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, IWW was in existence then anyway, you see, after the war. And before the war, when all the existence joined the Royal Legion of Lords and Lumberman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they were, what they wanted was good? The IWW?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They wanted a little bit too much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The company wasn't ready to give it to them. Now it's different. Now they sign a contract and they have to abide by the contract. But it isn't as strong as the IWW.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the Royal Legion of Lords and Lumberman, was that a good union?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, it was mostly for the company.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You know, it was to keep from starting and things like that.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The IWW, the Laugust and Lumberman, is the predecessor of the AFML. You see, after that was defunct. The AFML came in and they signed up a lot of people here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the company would have come around and given the eight -hour day and improved conditions unless the unions pushed them?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, well, I don't think so. No, the company wouldn't do that. But the unions did do some pushing.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The company wouldn't do that, no. Because they weren't meant to work ten hours so they can produce more lumber with cheaper wedges. But after they got the eight -hour work -wide, they had to raise their wedges too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: To some extent. And now the AFML is not as bad as when they first organized. They both affiliated with the AFML and the CIO. And that way they worked better. I think one is not against the other. And they're both working together against the company of large employers. You see, the small employers like the automobile industry and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked ten hours a day, was that very hard to do?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, at that time I was a young man. I didn't care. It wasn't very hard for me, you know. But now, of course, it will be too hard.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I wouldn't like to work ten hours a day now.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Neither would I, but I did. It was ten hours all over. You work in railroad ten hours.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And railroad was worse than the Somis, you see. Pick and so on. You had to work much a heck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like a what?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, you had to do the job, you see, to work pretty hard. And the boss was right there watching you.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Railroad was hard work at that time, ten hours a day. And hot weather. So one time we worked down to Snecke, down to Wallula and Walla Walla and Yuma Tila down through there. It was pretty hot in summertime. But that time I was 17, 18 years old. I didn't mind it so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any way to get in out of the heat? Is there any place to go?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no other place, no.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But of course, when I came to Somi here, after five years after that, I stayed with it. I thought it was a better job here anyway and better wedges. A little better, not very much. But common labor here was two dollars, and in railroads was a dollar seventy -five, a dollar and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how did it work?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the shift from one place to the other, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You told me before that the bosses looked down on the men and the railroad work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What way did they do that? Did you know that they looked down?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I'll tell you, the boss at that time had an interpreter, because he couldn't tell me to do something and I don't understand him, you see. And of course the interpreter explained it to me. Now he will tell the interpreter that I want that dog, such a thing. And he would come over there and tell the men in their own language what to do, what the boss wanted to do. But the boss was right there all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what you mean by he looked down at you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I'm not sure. That's what I mean, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was watching whether you work or not.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And if you don't work, he doesn't tell you anything. He tells me, he tells me because you don't understand.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah, these are hard days, them days, on railroad and everywhere else.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But them days are gone.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: For the present generation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those bosses, do you think they were, those bosses, were they fair or did they expect too much?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, they were not fair because they had to do, the more they did, you see, the better credit they get from the company. And now maybe they would advance. They get raised or they get a better job.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that was the, they make you work hard so they get the credit that they did. And some of them, of course, they were naturally devils. They were just hard men. He says, one boss down in California, he says, I want to see one Greek to die on the trudge from working. He was a, I don't know what the heck was his name, I can't remember now. Anyway, he was pretty rough.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I never worked in California myself, but that's what some of the fellows that came up from California was telling us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would guys do? Would they work for a man like that if he's trying to kill you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not exactly to kill you, but he'll work you to death, you see. I like to see one Greek die on the trudge. They were working, they were doing, yeah, well, they had to work because they couldn't do nothing else. And they had to work because if they didn't work, they didn't get paid. And they were hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made that work harder than the sawmill work? Why was it that much harder?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, because there's a fish over in the pig.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You had to show it down, you see, from the trudge, from the inside of the trudge, you had to throw it out. And then the razor tried to put it under the tiles. And the realers in the history knew.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You had to put a lot of gravel on in order to keep them even.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And it was to keep them even and to keep them steady, to stay, you see, when the train goes by there, it won't sink. If the ground is soft, the railroad will sink and the train will go out of the trudge. And if that happened, then it's damaging. My horse somebody, and if it's passenger train, they probably hold too many. And the company has to pay the damages.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So they had to wait. It was different. But now the railroads, they don't know the railroad business. And then they were the chief industry.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You mentioned something to me about you had to work in a mudslide once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was in, you know, 11 -word walking to this?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was up in the caskets, in the mountains. They had a big slide. So the whole mountain came down there, and you had to throw the mud and everything out. And sometimes you had to keep putting in a new truck. And they come up and got me and the other fellas from the section. You see, you were working in a section. And they didn't tell us where they're going and what is wrong or anything, because we don't understand them. We did tell us anyway.

speaker: Gus Demus

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Just depend on... Not down there in Portland. I mean, they weren't... Well, the crew split, you see, from... From down there to Fredo. They took us down to...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: ...Chihillis.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And... We worked while there. They were putting in double trudge. At that time. And it was in the wintertime, and it was more... more... it was terrible.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We worked a while, and then... In the wintertime...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They got pretty muddy, you know, and we quit a whole bunch.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I was quick.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I went to Portland. That's when I went to... This construction works. That was in 1911.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: From one construction to the other, and then I went back... Railroad. I worked on... I worked on a... Belt line, they call it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The hoodry, there is a little branch line... That goes up to...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The apple belt.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's up on the farms where there's... they grow the apples, you know. They grow a lot of apples in...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Hoodry, there. Hoodry grow apples there, they would know it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: For the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wenatchee, isn't it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But more so in Hootiever, they... it's...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Apple belt, I think, was called the little line. I worked on that there for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you just going to where the best money was and the best job you could find?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, the only job at that time, it was... that was the only job, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And some fella found the work, you know, and he needed a couple more men.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Myself and my cousin went over, two cousins. I had from Portland. And I had two cousins from... They were in Silverton, Oregon for many, many years. They died now. One was married. And had three boys, I think. Two boys with his first wife. And one boy with a second. He was married two times.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Pretty good man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he died here and he was younger man, didn't I?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He's called his brother. There's two brothers. His brother died before him and he was the oldest. And I worked there too in Silverton.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: On Redwood.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That was Logan Road. It wasn't much of a railroad but they were hauling logs, that's all. For the Silver Falls Timber Company.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The mill.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The mill was in Silverton. They had some big logs in these.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job there?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: On the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you saving money on all these different jobs or were you sort of just spending it as you made it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You could save very much. Because you worked, you know. You worked for a while and then you quit and you go down and you stay there for a while and you would like to spend money in town. And then you did another job. Well, I did save a little bit.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Enough to send my father back to Elkhonji because he was too old to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the working?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I gave him somebody who he left.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because he couldn't pay, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: $400, I don't know, $400 was a lot of money in those days.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And all I had, that's all I had, you see. I gave it to him too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want for me?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When you made money, you had money like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you just keep it with you? All that money?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. Yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: While I was traveling, now if I went to Poland, I put it in a bank. But when I was traveling from one place to the other, I kept it with me. And of course, when I came to Spokane, I... Let's see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I came to Badlads in 1914 from the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The branch line of the Hapner Junction, you know what Hapner is?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They were...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I worked over there for quite a while at Hapner, and then they laid us off. I and my brother.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And then I had three calls in share. They were a lot of them. They all called for me. Seven in town, but those were the calls, you see. And I kept corresponding with them, and they said, come on. So I came from Hapner Junction. I came to Colfax. And from Colfax to Moscow on one coach. One coach, it was from Colfax to Moscow. I don't remember what that one coach was for, but that's all there was, just one coach. Then it was its own machine, you see. It was run by electricity. I came to Badlads to Palouse from Moscow on Electric 10. At that time, I think it was the Roman Electric line from Moscow to Spokane.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't remember what that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the Inland Electric or the Spokane?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, I think it was the Inland. I think it was the Inland.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't remember. But anyway, we came in Palouse and from Palouse. At that time, they were running through from here to Palouse to Boville. You see, Washington, Idaho and Montana, this railroad here, they intended, when they built that, to run into Montana, but they didn't. They went up to, as far as Boville, and then they connected to the Milwaukee. Because the Milwaukee came from East over there to St. Mary's and then from St. Mary's to Boville, and they didn't have to build their own lines. And there were a lot of stockholders, you know, that they owned the Milwaukee.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that's why they called this side of Washington, right, of Atlanta.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I wanted to ask you about your father, too, when you were saying that he was, he got too old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the work over here hard on him because of his age?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah. It's really nice.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He couldn't work on the railroad.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We had, when I was, so, I can't...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Just a moth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can't see where it is, but it's flying inside, isn't it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Just a moth, that's all.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He's against the window.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, he couldn't work. We had him as a war boy, you see, in a gang of men.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that's all he was doing, you see, he was carrying war. They had what they called war boys. 20, 30 men, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he couldn't do any other work. It was too hard for him, so... There was a bunch of fellas going to Hulk Andre and he... He wrote me, he was here in Spokane, and I was in Portland that time. I was working in a white salmon in a dam. And I am giving money to go to your country.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that time was a war between the Malcolm States and Greece, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Greece and Bulgaria.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And there were a lot of young fellas, you know, from here they left. They went to war there. But I had sent the old man, they kid me, you know, when I called Martha to Spokane and said, you send your old man to fight in war, I said, yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they went all together, you see, at a company.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, it's been a pretty rough life, all right. But here in Parnach, I settled here and I... I made level one here, but I went back to Greece in 1962, 1961. And I found my sister, the widow, and two kids. I was in pretty bad shape.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I had the help, I couldn't...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: couldn't get away.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So... now it's too late to make money.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the inflation took quite a bit.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I had one house next to this here, that's where I was living before.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And when I came back, I bought this one here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And cost me twice as much as it did to remodel it, you see, it was... inside was no...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: nothing.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And cost me twice as much as it cost me for that. There too, you see, because the wedges were high and the materials high.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I bought the car in Moscow. That cost me a couple thousand dollars and I can't drive it now, I have to sell it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: If I can get anything out of it, I don't know. But... because it's...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: it's all, you know... fifty...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: fifty -nine model.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've seen it? Have you seen it? Is it out front? Is it out in front?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, it's in the garage. Okay, no, no. It's a caravina.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You should be able to sell it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, caravina, only thirty -five, thirty -six thousand miles. I don't know how to say much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You should be able to sell it and get a good price if it's got low mileage like that.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, they're looking model, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And if you're going to sell it to a garage, why they wouldn't give you nothing?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, sell it to a person. They wouldn't give you nothing anyway. I don't know what it's worth now because it's pretty old money. Did...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: had your father been here very long before he went back to the old country?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, he's been here before... before I was used...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: he came here in nineteen -seven and left in nineteen -twelve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seventy -twelve is five years, isn't it? Could he work when he first came over pretty good?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not very well. But he could get by, you know, with other men. All the same town, you see. They were...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He got worse, you know, as a... you know, he got... He got all... I don't remember how old he was.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Back in old country, he never worked, you see. He had a little store there and he... He was a businessman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, tavern and... It's mixed there, you know, with several kinds of things.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Few... clothing and... and so on and so forth, yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Do you know why... He decided to come here to make money, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, that's it, to make money?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He found that money is not easy, he is not grown in the trees. You see, you gotta work here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Did you think a lot of people thought that same thing, they could come over...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: and just make money easy?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Just come over and make money and go ahead and go back.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's what I thought. But when I came here, I found out different. And I decided that I will never go back anywhere. But I did go back on vision. And it cost me plenty.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But... In the last... In the last, you know... last call... I lived... if I lived two, three, or more years, why... that's all.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And if I go blind, I don't know what I'm going to do with that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean if you live two, three more years?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's all. Well...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: 83 years old and 23 years, maybe 86. I didn't get too old to live any longer. Of course, I lived to be 90, 100. I saw one man, a poor fellow, a old teacher from Moscow. He was 104 years old. He came to... Boise, from Ohio.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, of course, he had somebody with him. He didn't travel alone. Then went back when he was 100 years old and went back and lived four more years. And he died.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was a teacher.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He used to tell us about the old times, you know, how the teachers, how they walk. Or not black dirt, you know, between Moscow and Colfax.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They used to walk. They didn't have no automobiles, no... Not even... well, they had the boggies. But he didn't have one and he had to walk.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the government sent him to...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Hawaii... to teach English.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But he didn't like it. He came back and he went back to Ohio. I don't know how he called me. I went to Ohio. But he was a little bit more than I thought. That's why he died. Pretty nice, we'll follow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, Gus, that people had a very good chance to really get ahead and get ahead quite a bit? Or was it hard?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some people.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some people, by working, you can't get ahead, you see. It just makes living a toll. If you're in a business, you might get ahead. One fella, a Greek fella, he wasn't educated or anything. But he went to... From here he went to Spokane and he got in a business there, in that clubhouse there. And he lost what he had. He went back to work in the Somerville and made a few dollars and went to Seattle. And got into a partnership with another fella there. There was one place there that was ready to burst, you see. And he went in there. And he was lucky that the war started at the same time and he got out there with $100 ,000, believe it or not. I saw it with my own eyes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he wasn't smart or anything, but he just headed for the business. Because by working you can't make the money. You have to save it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Save it in England and still you don't get ahead. But there are a lot of fellas, a lot of Greek fellas that are doing business and they made money, others lost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it just luck that makes a person win or lose?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I think so. Of course you have to have experience in business. But that fella didn't have nothing, no experience, but he was pretty tight with his money and he made it. And he went back to Greece and got married and he came to New York and I don't know what happened from there on. And here he bought a apartment house over there. You know, a apartment house was getting a lot of guys that were known from the war country and others. It was a regular apartment house for anybody. And of course his wife was helped. And I don't know what happened. I haven't lost the trash of him, I haven't read or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would a little bit of money in the United States go a long ways in Greece for men to go back?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, not now. No, I mean in the early days.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In the early days, yes. But now everything is the same. You buy one pair of pants or one suit of clothes, you may have to pay a little cheaper but not very much. Because everything over there now is based on the dollar, on the price. Of the dollar.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they don't manufacture anything there anyway and all comes in either from England or France or United States. And they can't afford to sell it any cheaper because they sell it here because they have to pay the price to buy it. And now it's the dollar, it's the best of all occurrences in Europe. And especially in the southern Europe.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because Greece is a small country, you know, it's not... I don't produce anything, they have to import most everything and they have no export. They don't have nothing to sell in exchange, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: At first, years ago, they used to have something, they had what they call the...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The raisins.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The raisins there. Before they started raising in California. The raisins from Greece, you see. And they sold them to the United States and they were getting money, they were getting a pretty good price. And now, they raised everything in California. Raises, olive oil, olives and things, you know, that they raised over there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they don't get nothing here because California raises everything.
